title: Mince Pie Cookies
subtitle: Crisp at the edges, soft in the middle.
excerpt: Mince Pie Cookies. A baking recipe from Rebecca's collection, straightforward, reliable, worth weighing rather than scooping.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook, a recipe she's made many times in her own kitchen.

content.text: Mince Pie Cookies. An American family-table recipe, generous, comforting, built for a crowd. Adjustable for what you need.
Cookies bake fast and reward a chilled dough. Pull them out when the edges set and the middles still look slightly underdone.

"Crumbly and fragrant with fruit, I'd say these cookies have a good shot at converting people who don’t like mince pies," says Jamie. "Having a stash in the freezer will make your life so much easier during the festive madness. Seriously good stuff."

Preheat the oven to 180C/gas 4 and line 2 baking trays with greaseproof paper. Beat the butter and sugar together in a large bowl until creamy. Add the egg yolk and clementine zest and beat again to combine. Sift in the flour then fold through most of the mincemeat and stir until the mixture starts to come together, use your hands to make a dough, if needed.

Pull off little biscuit-sized clumps of dough, space them evenly over the trays and gently press down slightly to shape into cookies. Dot a little of your saved mince on top of each cookie to make them look extra delicious. Pop them in the oven for 10 minutes, or till golden but still a bit doughy in the middle.  Serve warm, or turn onto a wire rack to cool, then store in an airtight container.

If you don’t want to bake the whole batch, you can shape the dough into biscuits then freeze. Alternatively, shape into a log and slice of rounds as you want to bake them. Just pop into the oven at 180C/gas 4 for 10 to 15 minutes until golden and lovely.

attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.fix: Chill the dough for 30 minutes before baking. Preheat the oven properly, 180°C means 180°C on an oven thermometer, not the dial.
items.cause: Butter too soft, or oven not hot enough.
items.symptom: Cookies spread too thin and crisp

items.fix: Stop mixing as soon as the flour disappears. For chewier cookies, replace some plain flour with bread flour for more gluten.
items.cause: Over-mixed once the flour went in, or too much flour.
items.symptom: Cookies came out cakey, not chewy

items.fix: Leave them on the tray for five minutes after baking. They're too soft to move when hot. Lift onto a rack with a palette knife.
items.cause: Tried to lift before they'd set.

items.fix: Use a heavier baking tray, line with baking paper, and bake on the middle shelf.
items.cause: Tray too thin, or rack too low.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong

content.text: Add-ins: 75 g dark chocolate chips, chopped walnuts, dried cranberries, or a pinch of sea salt on top before baking.
Gluten-free: replace plain flour with a 1-to-1 gluten-free blend that includes xanthan gum.
Best fresh. Leftovers keep two days in the fridge; reheat covered with a splash of liquid.
A classic American home recipe, generous, comforting, made for a crowd. The proportions are heartier than the British equivalent; the method is built for a Sunday afternoon.
